Our story starts as many calamities due in 2019, when Milan and Cortina dem Pazo were selected as the host cities for the 2026 Winter Olympics. To their credit, they did have a plan to get a hockey arena built. Events company CTS Eventem would develop an arena in Santa Julia, Milan that the Olympics could lease as an ice hockey arena before it became a permanent concert venue. In fact, it was the only permanent venue plan for these games, which you think would mean there'd be time to get it done. But in 2020, the craziest thing happened. The pandemic, which to be fair delayed a lot of things, delayed plans to start building the venue and also drove up construction costs. Costs that were driven even higher by Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 and the war that ensued. The initial plan was to build a 210 ish $1,000,000 arena starting in the fall of 2022 and finishing by the fall of 2025. What they actually did was sort of build a nearly $300 million arena starting in spring of 2023 and finishing. We all hope eventually for the Olympics the arena needed two things. One, to be a functional arena with places for spectators, press players, etcetera to sit, eat, go to the bathroom and generally be encased within walls and roof. And 2A game ready ice hockey rink, which is no easy task. You need to build a big concrete slab with pipes inside to circulate liquid cold enough to freeze water without itself freezing, which can maintain a consistent temperature across its entire surface. Then you got to mist down and freeze dozens of millimeter thick layers of ice to make sure you end up with an appropriately hard air bubble free hockey, safe sheet of ice. Building a decent rink is obviously important on its own, but even more so here because the National Hockey League, whose season the Winter Olympics lands smack in the middle of, is lending their players out to the Olympics and threatened that they pulled him out if the Santa Julia ice wasn't up to snuff.

NHL have only been at 5 Olympics, with the most recent before 2026 being the 2014 Sochi Games. Since then, the NHL, the NHL players union, the international governing body of hockey, and the IOC couldn't come to an agreement that allowed NHL players to play. That's because letting them do so is, in many ways, not great for the league. The Olympics fall right in the middle of the regular NHL season and right after the end of the American football season, IE a great time to capture some wayward football fan eyeballs. In effectively leasing players to the Olympics, the NHL creates a huge gap in their schedule and takes on the risk that their best of the best players might get injured while they're there. Players that are being paid millions upon millions of dollars to play hockey in the NHL. So there's some hesitance to send their most valuable assets to Italy for three weeks. However, those valuable assets really vocally want to play for their national teams. Not to mention the Olympics fit nicely into the NHL stated desire to grow the game internationally. So with all that in mind, they reached an agreement for the Milan Olympics only to find out as of a few months before the 1st puck drop that the rank would be 3 feet shorter than they'd sign up for in the agreement. And also their owners weren't getting the hospitality suite on site they were promised, and also the arena wasn't done yet. Whoops.

The first Test matches at the Santa Julia Arena were supposed to be played in December of 2025, which is pretty late, by the way. The Bolshoi Ice Dome, built for 2014, hosted games as early as April 2013 and held an entire tournament in the December preceding the Olympics. But in December of 2025, not only were the Santa Julia Arena test games cancelled, presumably due to the place not being built yet, the NHL Board of Governors threatened to pull NHL players out of the Olympics if the ice wasn't safe to play on. Not that the players seem to care, but it's not up to them. And to be clear here, the NHL didn't want to pull players. In fact, when word got out about how not built to the arena was, the NHL sent some of their top ice technicians over to Italy to pull this thing together. The first skaters touched Santa Julia Arena ice on January 9th, when the still under construction arena hosted a handful of Italian Cup games to simulate Olympic Level 3 games a day ice use. At that point, just three of the Plant 14 ringside dressing rooms were ready. The Zamboni ramp was just two days old, and the first Test game was delayed by 5:00-ish minutes to repair a hole in the ice, reportedly part of a wider soft spot in front of one of the Nets. Over the course of three days, the ice held up and there were no further delays for hole patching.

And while players reported the ice was a bit uneven, with some spots harder or softer than others, everyone seemed to have walked away confident that the ice itself would be ready for Olympic play. All that was left was for Dom Moffett, the ice maker for both the Colorado Avalanche and the last five Olympic ice hockey rinks, to shave down the top layer, repaint the ice white and add the lines in the Olympic logo, then refreeze the new top layers. All of which he would usually do about two months beforehand. This time he got 3 weeks. The test games made people feel better about the ice and satisfy the NHL, but it did not necessarily inspire confidence in the rest of the arena.
